{{Lunisolar December}} {{Lunisolar February}} [[File:Eitaku Kotoosame.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Sensai Eitaku|ja|鮮斎永濯|12=draft}} ({{Year|1884}}) *Onko Nenju Gyōji*. Depiction of "Koto-osame" on {{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日|12=draft}}, with a hanging me-kago (eye basket).]]
'''Kotohachi-ka''' (事八日, "Eighth Day of Matters") or '''Koto Yōka''' is a traditional Japanese annual event. It is also referred to as '''Yōka Sekku''' (八日節供)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkamachi''' (八日待)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkazō''' (八日ぞう)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Koto Hajime''' (事始め)/'''Koto Osame''' (事納め)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''O-koto Hajime''' (お事始め)/'''O-koto Osame''' (お事納め)<ref>『{{ill|Kojien|ja|広辞苑|12=draft}}』 "Kotohachi-ka"</ref>, '''Yōkabuki''' (八日吹き), '''Yōkagyō''' (八日行), '''Sekku Hajime''' (節供始め)/'''Sekku Osame''' (節供納め), '''O-yakushi-sama''' (お薬師様), or '''Ebisu Kō''' (恵比寿講). In folklore studies, it is collectively referred to as '''Kotohachi-ka'''.<ref name=Nipponica/>
== Overview == Although there are exceptions, it is typically observed either on '''{{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日|12=draft}}''' or '''{{ill|December 8|ja|12月8日|12=draft}}''', or on both days in a year. In some regions, one date is called *Koto Hajime* ("Beginning of Matters"), while the other is called *Koto Osame* ("Ending of Matters"). The interpretation of "Koto" (事, matters) varies: in most regions, it refers to '''annual festivals or agricultural work''', making February 8 the "beginning" and December 8 the "end." However, in parts of '''{{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}''', "Koto" is understood as '''New Year celebrations''', meaning that '''December marks the beginning and February the end'''.<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf Tochigi Prefectural Customs and Language Collection] {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft}}, retrieved January 20, 2021.</ref>.
The content of the event varies significantly by region, and it has gradually declined since the postwar period.
== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|12=draft}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher={{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|12=draft}}|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - {{ill|Namioka, Aomori|ja|浪岡町|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hiraga, Hirosaki|ja|平賀町|12=draft}} in '''{{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft}}''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|12=draft}} in '''{{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|12=draft}}''' - {{ill|Sukagawa, Fukushima|ja|須賀川市|12=draft}} in '''{{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|12=draft}}'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher={{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学|12=draft}} |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''{{ill|Warabi, Saitama|ja|蕨市|12=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Toda, Saitama|ja|戸田市|12=draft}}''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Urawa-ku, Saitama|ja|浦和区|12=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Oshima, Tokyo|ja|大島町|12=draft}} ({{ill|Izu Ōshima|ja|伊豆大島|12=draft}})''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Sōka, Saitama|ja|草加市|12=draft}}''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Misato, Saitama|ja|三郷市|12=draft}}''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Yamatsuri, Fukushima|ja|矢祭町|12=draft}}''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Hanawa, Fukushima|ja|塙町|12=draft}}''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - {{ill|Hitotsume-kozō|ja|一つ目小僧|12=draft}} - {{ill|Mikakaribaba|ja|箕借り婆|12=draft}} - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神|12=draft}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯|12=draft}})
Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ({{ill|Ebisu|ja|えびす|12=draft}}) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ({{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来|12=draft}}) * '''Daikokuten''' ({{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|12=draft}}) * '''Ta no Kami''' ({{ill|Field deity|ja|田の神|12=draft}}) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|12=draft}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ({{ill|Hari kuyō|ja|針供養|12=draft}}) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|12=draft}} * '''February 8''' in {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|12=draft}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.

== Overview == Although there are exceptions, it is typically observed either on '''{{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日|12=draft}}''' or '''{{ill|December 8|ja|12月8日|12=draft}}''', or on both days in a year. In some regions, one date is called *Koto Hajime* ("Beginning of Matters"), while the other is called *Koto Osame* ("Ending of Matters"). The interpretation of "Koto" (事, matters) varies: in most regions, it refers to '''annual festivals or agricultural work''', making February 8 the "beginning" and December 8 the "end." However, in parts of '''{{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}''', "Koto" is understood as '''New Year celebrations''', meaning that '''December marks the beginning and February the end'''.<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf Tochigi Prefectural Customs and Language Collection] {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft}}, retrieved January 20, 2021.</ref>.
The content of the event varies significantly by region, and it has gradually declined since the postwar period.
== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|12=draft}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher={{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|12=draft}}|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - {{ill|Namioka, Aomori|ja|浪岡町|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hiraga, Hirosaki|ja|平賀町|12=draft}} in '''{{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft}}''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|12=draft}} in '''{{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|12=draft}}''' - {{ill|Sukagawa, Fukushima|ja|須賀川市|12=draft}} in '''{{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|12=draft}}'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher={{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学|12=draft}} |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''{{ill|Warabi, Saitama|ja|蕨市|12=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Toda, Saitama|ja|戸田市|12=draft}}''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Urawa-ku, Saitama|ja|浦和区|12=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Oshima, Tokyo|ja|大島町|12=draft}} ({{ill|Izu Ōshima|ja|伊豆大島|12=draft}})''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Sōka, Saitama|ja|草加市|12=draft}}''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Misato, Saitama|ja|三郷市|12=draft}}''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Yamatsuri, Fukushima|ja|矢祭町|12=draft}}''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Hanawa, Fukushima|ja|塙町|12=draft}}''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - {{ill|Hitotsume-kozō|ja|一つ目小僧|12=draft}} - {{ill|Mikakaribaba|ja|箕借り婆|12=draft}} - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神|12=draft}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯|12=draft}})
Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ({{ill|Ebisu|ja|えびす|12=draft}}) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ({{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来|12=draft}}) * '''Daikokuten''' ({{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|12=draft}}) * '''Ta no Kami''' ({{ill|Field deity|ja|田の神|12=draft}}) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|12=draft}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ({{ill|Hari kuyō|ja|針供養|12=draft}}) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|12=draft}} * '''February 8''' in {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|12=draft}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Hari kuyō|ja|針供養|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|ja|正月事始め|12=draft}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese annual events]] [[Category:February]] [[Category:December]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Lunisolar December]] [[Category:Lunisolar February]] }} [[sn:Kotohachi-ka]] [[ja:事八日]] [[Category:Lunisolar December]] [[Category:Lunisolar February]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kotohachi-ka]][[Category:qq]]

{{Lunisolar December}} {{Lunisolar February}} [[File:Eitaku Kotoosame.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Sensai Eitaku|ja|鮮斎永濯|12=draft}} ({{Year|1884}}) *Onko Nenju Gyōji*. Depiction of "Koto-osame" on {{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日|12=draft}}, with a hanging me-kago (eye basket).]]
'''Kotohachi-ka''' (事八日, "Eighth Day of Matters") or '''Koto Yōka''' is a traditional Japanese annual event. It is also referred to as '''Yōka Sekku''' (八日節供)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkamachi''' (八日待)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkazō''' (八日ぞう)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Koto Hajime''' (事始め)/'''Koto Osame''' (事納め)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''O-koto Hajime''' (お事始め)/'''O-koto Osame''' (お事納め)<ref>『{{ill|Kojien|ja|広辞苑|12=draft}}』 "Kotohachi-ka"</ref>, '''Yōkabuki''' (八日吹き), '''Yōkagyō''' (八日行), '''Sekku Hajime''' (節供始め)/'''Sekku Osame''' (節供納め), '''O-yakushi-sama''' (お薬師様), or '''Ebisu Kō''' (恵比寿講). In folklore studies, it is collectively referred to as '''Kotohachi-ka'''.<ref name=Nipponica/>
== Overview == Although there are exceptions, it is typically observed either on '''{{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日|12=draft}}''' or '''{{ill|December 8|ja|12月8日|12=draft}}''', or on both days in a year. In some regions, one date is called *Koto Hajime* ("Beginning of Matters"), while the other is called *Koto Osame* ("Ending of Matters"). The interpretation of "Koto" (事, matters) varies: in most regions, it refers to '''annual festivals or agricultural work''', making February 8 the "beginning" and December 8 the "end." However, in parts of '''{{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}''', "Koto" is understood as '''New Year celebrations''', meaning that '''December marks the beginning and February the end'''.<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf Tochigi Prefectural Customs and Language Collection] {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft}}, retrieved January 20, 2021.</ref>.
The content of the event varies significantly by region, and it has gradually declined since the postwar period.
== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|12=draft}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher={{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|12=draft}}|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - {{ill|Namioka, Aomori|ja|浪岡町|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hiraga, Hirosaki|ja|平賀町|12=draft}} in '''{{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft}}''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|12=draft}} in '''{{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|12=draft}}''' - {{ill|Sukagawa, Fukushima|ja|須賀川市|12=draft}} in '''{{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|12=draft}}'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher={{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学|12=draft}} |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''{{ill|Warabi, Saitama|ja|蕨市|12=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Toda, Saitama|ja|戸田市|12=draft}}''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Urawa-ku, Saitama|ja|浦和区|12=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Oshima, Tokyo|ja|大島町|12=draft}} ({{ill|Izu Ōshima|ja|伊豆大島|12=draft}})''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Sōka, Saitama|ja|草加市|12=draft}}''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Misato, Saitama|ja|三郷市|12=draft}}''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Yamatsuri, Fukushima|ja|矢祭町|12=draft}}''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Hanawa, Fukushima|ja|塙町|12=draft}}''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - {{ill|Hitotsume-kozō|ja|一つ目小僧|12=draft}} - {{ill|Mikakaribaba|ja|箕借り婆|12=draft}} - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神|12=draft}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯|12=draft}})

Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.

{{Lunisolar December}} {{Lunisolar February}} [[File:Eitaku Kotoosame.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Sensai Eitaku|ja|鮮斎永濯|12=draft}} ({{Year|1884}}) *Onko Nenju Gyōji*. Depiction of "Koto-osame" on {{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日|12=draft}}, with a hanging me-kago (eye basket).]]
'''Kotohachi-ka''' (事八日, "Eighth Day of Matters") or '''Koto Yōka''' is a traditional Japanese annual event. It is also referred to as '''Yōka Sekku''' (八日節供)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkamachi''' (八日待)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkazō''' (八日ぞう)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Koto Hajime''' (事始め)/'''Koto Osame''' (事納め)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''O-koto Hajime''' (お事始め)/'''O-koto Osame''' (お事納め)<ref>『{{ill|Kojien|ja|広辞苑|12=draft}}』 "Kotohachi-ka"</ref>, '''Yōkabuki''' (八日吹き), '''Yōkagyō''' (八日行), '''Sekku Hajime''' (節供始め)/'''Sekku Osame''' (節供納め), '''O-yakushi-sama''' (お薬師様), or '''Ebisu Kō''' (恵比寿講). In folklore studies, it is collectively referred to as '''Kotohachi-ka'''.<ref name=Nipponica/>
== Overview == Although there are exceptions, it is typically observed either on '''{{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日|12=draft}}''' or '''{{ill|December 8|ja|12月8日|12=draft}}''', or on both days in a year. In some regions, one date is called *Koto Hajime* ("Beginning of Matters"), while the other is called *Koto Osame* ("Ending of Matters"). The interpretation of "Koto" (事, matters) varies: in most regions, it refers to '''annual festivals or agricultural work''', making February 8 the "beginning" and December 8 the "end." However, in parts of '''{{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}''', "Koto" is understood as '''New Year celebrations''', meaning that '''December marks the beginning and February the end'''.<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf Tochigi Prefectural Customs and Language Collection] {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft}}, retrieved January 20, 2021.</ref>.
The content of the event varies significantly by region, and it has gradually declined since the postwar period.
== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|12=draft}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher={{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|12=draft}}|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - {{ill|Namioka, Aomori|ja|浪岡町|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hiraga, Hirosaki|ja|平賀町|12=draft}} in '''{{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft}}''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|12=draft}} in '''{{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|12=draft}}''' - {{ill|Sukagawa, Fukushima|ja|須賀川市|12=draft}} in '''{{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|12=draft}}'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher={{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学|12=draft}} |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''{{ill|Warabi, Saitama|ja|蕨市|12=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Toda, Saitama|ja|戸田市|12=draft}}''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Urawa-ku, Saitama|ja|浦和区|12=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Oshima, Tokyo|ja|大島町|12=draft}} ({{ill|Izu Ōshima|ja|伊豆大島|12=draft}})''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Sōka, Saitama|ja|草加市|12=draft}}''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Misato, Saitama|ja|三郷市|12=draft}}''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Yamatsuri, Fukushima|ja|矢祭町|12=draft}}''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Hanawa, Fukushima|ja|塙町|12=draft}}''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - {{ill|Hitotsume-kozō|ja|一つ目小僧|12=draft}} - {{ill|Mikakaribaba|ja|箕借り婆|12=draft}} - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神|12=draft}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯|12=draft}})
Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ({{ill|Ebisu|ja|えびす|12=draft}}) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ({{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|12=draft}}) * '''Daikokuten''' ({{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|12=draft}}) * '''Ta no Kami''' ({{ill|Field deity|ja|田の神|12=draft}}) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|12=draft}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ({{ill|Hari kuyō|ja|針供養|12=draft}}) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|12=draft}} * '''February 8''' in {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|12=draft}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Hari kuyō|ja|針供養|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|ja|正月事始め|12=draft}}

{{Lunisolar December}} {{Lunisolar February}} [[File:Eitaku Kotoosame.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Sensai Eitaku|ja|鮮斎永濯|12=draft}} ({{Year|1884}}) *Onko Nenju Gyōji*. Depiction of "Koto-osame" on {{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日|12=draft}}, with a hanging me-kago (eye basket).]]

[[sn:Kotohachi-ka]] [[ja:事八日]] [[ar:Kotohachi-ka]]

{{Lunisolar December}} {{Lunisolar February}} [[File:Eitaku Kotoosame.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Sensai Eitaku|ja|鮮斎永濯}} ({{Year|1884}}) *Onko Nenju Gyōji*. Depiction of "Koto-osame" on {{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日}}, with a hanging me-kago (eye basket).]]
'''Kotohachi-ka''' (事八日, "Eighth Day of Matters") or '''Koto Yōka''' is a traditional Japanese annual event. It is also referred to as '''Yōka Sekku''' (八日節供)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkamachi''' (八日待)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkazō''' (八日ぞう)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Koto Hajime''' (事始め)/'''Koto Osame''' (事納め)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''O-koto Hajime''' (お事始め)/'''O-koto Osame''' (お事納め)<ref>『{{ill|Kojien|ja|広辞苑}}』 "Kotohachi-ka"</ref>, '''Yōkabuki''' (八日吹き), '''Yōkagyō''' (八日行), '''Sekku Hajime''' (節供始め)/'''Sekku Osame''' (節供納め), '''O-yakushi-sama''' (お薬師様), or '''Ebisu Kō''' (恵比寿講). In folklore studies, it is collectively referred to as '''Kotohachi-ka'''.<ref name=Nipponica/>
== Overview == Although there are exceptions, it is typically observed either on '''{{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日}}''' or '''{{ill|December 8|ja|12月8日}}''', or on both days in a year. In some regions, one date is called *Koto Hajime* ("Beginning of Matters"), while the other is called *Koto Osame* ("Ending of Matters"). The interpretation of "Koto" (事, matters) varies: in most regions, it refers to '''annual festivals or agricultural work''', making February 8 the "beginning" and December 8 the "end." However, in parts of '''{{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方}}''', "Koto" is understood as '''New Year celebrations''', meaning that '''December marks the beginning and February the end'''.<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf Tochigi Prefectural Customs and Language Collection] {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}}, retrieved January 20, 2021.</ref>.
The content of the event varies significantly by region, and it has gradually declined since the postwar period.
== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher={{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - {{ill|Namioka, Aomori|ja|浪岡町}} and {{ill|Hiraga, Hirosaki|ja|平賀町}} in '''{{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下}} in '''{{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}}''' - {{ill|Sukagawa, Fukushima|ja|須賀川市}} in '''{{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher={{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学}} |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''{{ill|Warabi, Saitama|ja|蕨市}}''' and '''{{ill|Toda, Saitama|ja|戸田市}}''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Urawa-ku, Saitama|ja|浦和区}}''' and '''{{ill|Oshima, Tokyo|ja|大島町}} ({{ill|Izu Ōshima|ja|伊豆大島}})''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Sōka, Saitama|ja|草加市}}''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Misato, Saitama|ja|三郷市}}''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Yamatsuri, Fukushima|ja|矢祭町}}''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Hanawa, Fukushima|ja|塙町}}''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - {{ill|Hitotsume-kozō|ja|一つ目小僧}} - {{ill|Mikakaribaba|ja|箕借り婆}} - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯}})
Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ({{ill|Ebisu|ja|えびす}}) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ({{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha}}) * '''Daikokuten''' ({{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天}}) * '''Ta no Kami''' ({{ill|Field deity|ja|田の神}}) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ({{ill|Hari kuyō|ja|針供養|1=Hari-Kuyō|lt=Needle Memorial Service}}) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方}} * '''February 8''' in {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方}}<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Hari kuyō|ja|針供養|1=Hari-Kuyō|lt=Needle Memorial Service}} * {{ill|Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|ja|正月事始め}}

revision.text:
[[File:Eitaku Kotoosame.jpg|thumb|[[鮮斎永濯]] ({{年|1884}}) 『温古年中行事』。[[2月8日]]の「事納」に目籠を掲げている。]]
'''事八日'''・'''コト八日'''（ことようか）は、日本の年中行事。'''八日節供'''<ref name=Nipponica/>、'''八日待'''（まち）<ref name=Nipponica/>、'''八日ぞう'''<ref name=Nipponica/>、'''事始め'''/'''事納め'''<ref name=Nipponica/>、'''お事始め'''/'''お事納め'''<ref>『[[広辞苑]]』事八日</ref>、'''八日吹き'''、'''八日行'''、'''節供始め'''/'''節供納め'''、'''お薬師様'''、'''恵比寿講'''などとも呼ばれるが、[[民俗学]]上は「事八日」と総称される<ref name=Nipponica/>。
== 概要 == 例外もあるが原則として、[[2月8日]]と[[12月8日]]の年2回またはいずれかに行われる、2月8日と12月8日のどちらかを「事始め」、他方を「事納め」と呼ぶことがある。「事」を年間の祭事あるいは農作業と解釈し、2月を事始め・12月を事納めとするのが主流だが、[[関東地方|関東]]の一部では「事」を[[正月|新年]]の祝い事（正月行事）と解釈し、12月が事始め・2月が事納めとなる<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf とちぎの慣習・ことば集] [[栃木県]]、2021年1月20日閲覧。</ref>。
行事の内容は地域差が大きい。戦後徐々に行われなくなりつつある。
== 日付 == 地域によっては、[[2月8日]]か[[12月8日]]の片方のみ行う。[[中部地方]]以東では両日行うが、[[北陸地方]]から西日本では、12月が主。特に[[近畿地方|近畿]]・[[中国地方]]では、[[3月 (旧暦)|旧暦3月]]ごろの[[春事]]（はるごと）に吸収され、12月のみ行うことが多い<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher=[[小学館]]|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>。
本来は日付は[[中国暦|旧暦]]で、[[青森県]][[青森市]][[浪岡町|浪岡]]、[[平川市]][[平賀町|平賀地域]]、[[山形県]][[朝日町 (山形県)|朝日町]][[宮宿 (朝日町)|宮宿]]、[[河北町]][[谷地 (河北町)|谷地]]、[[村山市]][[稲下]]、[[福島県]][[須賀川市]]などでは旧暦で行われる（ただし1970年代ごろの情報であり現状は不明）<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher=早稲田大学 |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>。
2月と12月以外に行われる地域もある。[[埼玉県]][[蕨市]]、[[戸田市]]では、2月8日と[[11月8日]]に行われる<ref name=Cho/>。埼玉県[[さいたま市]][[浦和区]]、[[東京都]][[大島町 ]]（[[伊豆大島]]）では、2月8日・[[4月8日]]・12月8日の年3回行われる<ref name=Cho/>。そのほか、埼玉県[[草加市]]では[[1月8日]]・2月8日・[[3月8日]]・[[4月8日]]・12月8日、[[三郷市]]では2月8日・[[4月8日]]・[[7月8日]]・[[10月8日]]・12月8日に行われることがある（必ずしもこれらの全ての日に行うということではない）<ref name=Cho/>。
福島県[[矢祭町]]では、各月の8日または10日に行われる<ref name=Cho/>。福島県[[塙町]]では、8日に目籠（後述）を掲げ10日にしまうなど、8日と10日に分けて行われる<ref name=Cho/>。
== 行事 == 妖怪や悪神（地域によって異なり、[[一つ目小僧]]・[[箕借り婆]]・[[疫病神]]・[[ダイマナコ]] など）が家を訪れ、害をなすという。それを防ぐため、[[目籠]]・[[ハリセンボン]]・[[蜂]]の巣・[[ヨモギ]]・[[山椒]]・[[唐辛子]]・[[ニンニク]]・[[柊鰯|柊に刺した鰯の頭]]などを、戸口や軒下に掲げ[[魔除け]]とする。
あるいは、[[えびす|恵比寿]]・[[薬師如来]]・[[大黒天|大黒]]・[[田の神]]・[[山の神]]などが訪れるといい、[[赤飯]]・[[餅]]・[[団子]]などを家に[[お供え|供え]]て歓迎する。
[[栃木県]]南部と[[茨城県]]南西部の一帯では[[笹]]を束ねた「笹神様」を庭に掲げる風習がみられ「北関東のササガミ習俗」として国の文化財に選択されている<ref name="tochigi" />。
[[針供養]]、すなわち、[[縫い針]]が[[供養]]される。[[関西地方|関西]]では12月8日、関東では2月8日が多い<ref name="heibon">『[[国民百科事典]]』平凡社 6巻 pp.198–199</ref>。
農作業をしてはならない、山に入ってはいけないなどの[[物忌]]がある。
== 出典 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[針供養]] * [[正月事始め]]

[[File:Eitaku Kotoosame.jpg|thumb|[[鮮斎永濯]] ({{年|1884}}) 『温古年中行事』。[[2月8日]]の「事納」に目籠を掲げている。]]
'''事八日'''・'''コト八日'''（ことようか）は、日本の年中行事。'''八日節供'''<ref name=Nipponica/>、'''八日待'''（まち）<ref name=Nipponica/>、'''八日ぞう'''<ref name=Nipponica/>、'''事始め'''/'''事納め'''<ref name=Nipponica/>、'''お事始め'''/'''お事納め'''<ref>『[[広辞苑]]』事八日</ref>、'''八日吹き'''、'''八日行'''、'''節供始め'''/'''節供納め'''、'''お薬師様'''、'''恵比寿講'''などとも呼ばれるが、[[民俗学]]上は「事八日」と総称される<ref name=Nipponica/>。
== 概要 == 例外もあるが原則として、[[2月8日]]と[[12月8日]]の年2回またはいずれかに行われる、2月8日と12月8日のどちらかを「事始め」、他方を「事納め」と呼ぶことがある。「事」を年間の祭事あるいは農作業と解釈し、2月を事始め・12月を事納めとするのが主流だが、[[関東地方|関東]]の一部では「事」を[[正月|新年]]の祝い事（正月行事）と解釈し、12月が事始め・2月が事納めとなる<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf とちぎの慣習・ことば集] [[栃木県]]、2021年1月20日閲覧。</ref>。
行事の内容は地域差が大きい。戦後徐々に行われなくなりつつある。
== 日付 == 地域によっては、[[2月8日]]か[[12月8日]]の片方のみ行う。[[中部地方]]以東では両日行うが、[[北陸地方]]から西日本では、12月が主。特に[[近畿地方|近畿]]・[[中国地方]]では、[[3月 (旧暦)|旧暦3月]]ごろの[[春事]]（はるごと）に吸収され、12月のみ行うことが多い<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher=[[小学館]]|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>。
本来は日付は[[中国暦|旧暦]]で、[[青森県]][[青森市]][[浪岡町|浪岡]]、[[平川市]][[平賀町|平賀地域]]、[[山形県]][[朝日町 (山形県)|朝日町]][[宮宿 (朝日町)|宮宿]]、[[河北町]][[谷地 (河北町)|谷地]]、[[村山市]][[稲下]]、[[福島県]][[須賀川市]]などでは旧暦で行われる（ただし1970年代ごろの情報であり現状は不明）<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher=早稲田大学 |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>。
2月と12月以外に行われる地域もある。[[埼玉県]][[蕨市]]、[[戸田市]]では、2月8日と[[11月8日]]に行われる<ref name=Cho/>。埼玉県[[さいたま市]][[浦和区]]、[[東京都]][[大島町 ]]（[[伊豆大島]]）では、2月8日・[[4月8日]]・12月8日の年3回行われる<ref name=Cho/>。そのほか、埼玉県[[草加市]]では[[1月8日]]・2月8日・[[3月8日]]・[[4月8日]]・12月8日、[[三郷市]]では2月8日・[[4月8日]]・[[7月8日]]・[[10月8日]]・12月8日に行われることがある（必ずしもこれらの全ての日に行うということではない）<ref name=Cho/>。
福島県[[矢祭町]]では、各月の8日または10日に行われる<ref name=Cho/>。福島県[[塙町]]では、8日に目籠（後述）を掲げ10日にしまうなど、8日と10日に分けて行われる<ref name=Cho/>。
== 行事 == 妖怪や悪神（地域によって異なり、[[一つ目小僧]]・[[箕借り婆]]・[[疫病神]]・[[ダイマナコ]] など）が家を訪れ、害をなすという。それを防ぐため、[[目籠]]・[[ハリセンボン]]・[[蜂]]の巣・[[ヨモギ]]・[[山椒]]・[[唐辛子]]・[[ニンニク]]・[[柊鰯|柊に刺した鰯の頭]]などを、戸口や軒下に掲げ[[魔除け]]とする。
あるいは、[[えびす|恵比寿]]・[[薬師如来]]・[[大黒天|大黒]]・[[田の神]]・[[山の神]]などが訪れるといい、[[赤飯]]・[[餅]]・[[団子]]などを家に[[お供え|供え]]て歓迎する。
[[栃木県]]南部と[[茨城県]]南西部の一帯では[[笹]]を束ねた「笹神様」を庭に掲げる風習がみられ「北関東のササガミ習俗」として国の文化財に選択されている<ref name="tochigi" />。

農作業をしてはならない、山に入ってはいけないなどの[[物忌]]がある。

{{Lunisolar December}} {{Lunisolar February}} [[File:Eitaku Kotoosame.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Sensai Eitaku|ja|鮮斎永濯|12=draft}} ({{Year|1884}}) *Onko Nenju Gyōji*. Depiction of "Koto-osame" on {{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日|12=draft}}, with a hanging me-kago (eye basket).]]
'''Kotohachi-ka''' (事八日, "Eighth Day of Matters") or '''Koto Yōka''' is a traditional Japanese annual event. It is also referred to as '''Yōka Sekku''' (八日節供)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkamachi''' (八日待)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkazō''' (八日ぞう)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Koto Hajime''' (事始め)/'''Koto Osame''' (事納め)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''O-koto Hajime''' (お事始め)/'''O-koto Osame''' (お事納め)<ref>『{{ill|Kojien|ja|広辞苑|12=draft}}』 "Kotohachi-ka"</ref>, '''Yōkabuki''' (八日吹き), '''Yōkagyō''' (八日行), '''Sekku Hajime''' (節供始め)/'''Sekku Osame''' (節供納め), '''O-yakushi-sama''' (お薬師様), or '''Ebisu Kō''' (恵比寿講). In folklore studies, it is collectively referred to as '''Kotohachi-ka'''.<ref name=Nipponica/>
== Overview == Although there are exceptions, it is typically observed either on '''{{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日|12=draft}}''' or '''{{ill|December 8|ja|12月8日|12=draft}}''', or on both days in a year. In some regions, one date is called *Koto Hajime* ("Beginning of Matters"), while the other is called *Koto Osame* ("Ending of Matters"). The interpretation of "Koto" (事, matters) varies: in most regions, it refers to '''annual festivals or agricultural work''', making February 8 the "beginning" and December 8 the "end." However, in parts of '''{{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}''', "Koto" is understood as '''New Year celebrations''', meaning that '''December marks the beginning and February the end'''.<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf Tochigi Prefectural Customs and Language Collection] {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft}}, retrieved January 20, 2021.</ref>.
The content of the event varies significantly by region, and it has gradually declined since the postwar period.
== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|12=draft}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher={{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|12=draft}}|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - {{ill|Namioka, Aomori|ja|浪岡町|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hiraga, Hirosaki|ja|平賀町|12=draft}} in '''{{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft}}''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|12=draft}} in '''{{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|12=draft}}''' - {{ill|Sukagawa, Fukushima|ja|須賀川市|12=draft}} in '''{{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|12=draft}}'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher={{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学|12=draft}} |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''{{ill|Warabi, Saitama|ja|蕨市|12=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Toda, Saitama|ja|戸田市|12=draft}}''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Urawa-ku, Saitama|ja|浦和区|12=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Oshima, Tokyo|ja|大島町|12=draft}} ({{ill|Izu Ōshima|ja|伊豆大島|12=draft}})''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Sōka, Saitama|ja|草加市|12=draft}}''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Misato, Saitama|ja|三郷市|12=draft}}''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Yamatsuri, Fukushima|ja|矢祭町|12=draft}}''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Hanawa, Fukushima|ja|塙町|12=draft}}''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - {{ill|Hitotsume-kozō|ja|一つ目小僧|12=draft}} - {{ill|Mikakaribaba|ja|箕借り婆|12=draft}} - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神|12=draft}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯|12=draft}})
Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ({{ill|Ebisu|ja|えびす|12=draft}}) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ({{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|12=draft}}) * '''Daikokuten''' ({{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|12=draft}}) * '''Ta no Kami''' ({{ill|Field deity|ja|田の神|12=draft}}) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|12=draft}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ({{ill|Hari kuyō|ja|針供養|1=Hari-Kuyō|lt=Needle Memorial Service|12=draft}}) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|12=draft}} * '''February 8''' in {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|12=draft}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.

[[sn:Kotohachi-ka]] [[ja:事八日]] [[ar:Kotohachi-ka]]

{{Lunisolar December}} {{Lunisolar February}} [[File:Eitaku Kotoosame.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Sensai Eitaku|ja|鮮斎永濯|qq=draft}} ({{Year|1884}}) *Onko Nenju Gyōji*. Depiction of "Koto-osame" on {{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日|qq=draft}}, with a hanging me-kago (eye basket).]]
'''Kotohachi-ka''' (事八日, "Eighth Day of Matters") or '''Koto Yōka''' is a traditional Japanese annual event. It is also referred to as '''Yōka Sekku''' (八日節供)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkamachi''' (八日待)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkazō''' (八日ぞう)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Koto Hajime''' (事始め)/'''Koto Osame''' (事納め)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''O-koto Hajime''' (お事始め)/'''O-koto Osame''' (お事納め)<ref>『{{ill|Kojien|ja|広辞苑|qq=draft}}』 "Kotohachi-ka"</ref>, '''Yōkabuki''' (八日吹き), '''Yōkagyō''' (八日行), '''Sekku Hajime''' (節供始め)/'''Sekku Osame''' (節供納め), '''O-yakushi-sama''' (お薬師様), or '''Ebisu Kō''' (恵比寿講). In folklore studies, it is collectively referred to as '''Kotohachi-ka'''.<ref name=Nipponica/>
== Overview == Although there are exceptions, it is typically observed either on '''{{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日|qq=draft}}''' or '''{{ill|December 8|ja|12月8日|qq=draft}}''', or on both days in a year. In some regions, one date is called *Koto Hajime* ("Beginning of Matters"), while the other is called *Koto Osame* ("Ending of Matters"). The interpretation of "Koto" (事, matters) varies: in most regions, it refers to '''annual festivals or agricultural work''', making February 8 the "beginning" and December 8 the "end." However, in parts of '''{{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft}}''', "Koto" is understood as '''New Year celebrations''', meaning that '''December marks the beginning and February the end'''.<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf Tochigi Prefectural Customs and Language Collection] {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|qq=draft}}, retrieved January 20, 2021.</ref>.
The content of the event varies significantly by region, and it has gradually declined since the postwar period.
== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|qq=draft}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher={{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|qq=draft}}|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - {{ill|Namioka, Aomori|ja|浪岡町|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hiraga, Hirosaki|ja|平賀町|qq=draft}} in '''{{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft}}''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|qq=draft}} in '''{{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|qq=draft}}''' - {{ill|Sukagawa, Fukushima|ja|須賀川市|qq=draft}} in '''{{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|qq=draft}}'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher={{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学|qq=draft}} |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''{{ill|Warabi, Saitama|ja|蕨市|qq=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Toda, Saitama|ja|戸田市|qq=draft}}''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Urawa-ku, Saitama|ja|浦和区|qq=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Oshima, Tokyo|ja|大島町|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Izu Ōshima|ja|伊豆大島|qq=draft}})''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Sōka, Saitama|ja|草加市|qq=draft}}''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Misato, Saitama|ja|三郷市|qq=draft}}''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Yamatsuri, Fukushima|ja|矢祭町|qq=draft}}''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Hanawa, Fukushima|ja|塙町|qq=draft}}''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - {{ill|Hitotsume-kozō|ja|一つ目小僧|qq=draft}} - {{ill|Mikakaribaba|ja|箕借り婆|qq=draft}} - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神|qq=draft}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯|qq=draft}})
Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ({{ill|Ebisu|ja|えびす|qq=draft}}) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ({{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft}}) * '''Daikokuten''' ({{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|qq=draft}}) * '''Ta no Kami''' ({{ill|Field deity|ja|田の神|qq=draft}}) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|qq=draft}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ({{ill|Hari kuyō|ja|針供養|1=Hari-Kuyō|lt=Needle Memorial Service|qq=draft}}) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft}} * '''February 8''' in {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft}}<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|qq=draft}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.

In some regions, one date is called *Koto Hajime* ("Beginning of Matters"), while the other is called *Koto Osame* ("Ending of Matters"). The interpretation of "Koto" (事, matters) varies: in most regions, it refers to '''annual festivals or agricultural work''', making February 8 the "beginning" and December 8 the "end." However, in parts of '''{{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft}}''', "Koto" is understood as '''New Year celebrations''', meaning that '''December marks the beginning and February the end'''.<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf Tochigi Prefectural Customs and Language Collection] {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|qq=draft}}, retrieved January 20, 2021.</ref>.
The content of the event varies significantly by region, and it has gradually declined since the postwar period.
== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|qq=draft}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher={{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|qq=draft}}|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - {{ill|Namioka, Aomori|ja|浪岡町|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hiraga, Hirosaki|ja|平賀町|qq=draft}} in '''{{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft}}''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|qq=draft}} in '''{{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|qq=draft}}''' - {{ill|Sukagawa, Fukushima|ja|須賀川市|qq=draft}} in '''{{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|qq=draft}}'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher={{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学|qq=draft}} |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''{{ill|Warabi, Saitama|ja|蕨市|qq=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Toda, Saitama|ja|戸田市|qq=draft}}''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Urawa-ku, Saitama|ja|浦和区|qq=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Oshima, Tokyo|ja|大島町|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Izu Ōshima|ja|伊豆大島|qq=draft}})''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Sōka, Saitama|ja|草加市|qq=draft}}''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Misato, Saitama|ja|三郷市|qq=draft}}''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Yamatsuri, Fukushima|ja|矢祭町|qq=draft}}''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Hanawa, Fukushima|ja|塙町|qq=draft}}''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - {{ill|Hitotsume-kozō|ja|一つ目小僧|qq=draft}} - {{ill|Mikakaribaba|ja|箕借り婆|qq=draft}} - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神|qq=draft}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯|qq=draft}})
Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ({{ill|Ebisu|ja|えびす|qq=draft}}) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ({{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft}}) * '''Daikokuten''' ({{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|qq=draft}}) * '''Ta no Kami''' ({{ill|Field deity|ja|田の神|qq=draft}}) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|qq=draft}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ({{ill|Hari kuyō|ja|針供養|1=Hari-Kuyō|lt=Needle Memorial Service|qq=draft}}) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft}} * '''February 8''' in {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft}}<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|qq=draft}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Hari kuyō|ja|針供養|1=Hari-Kuyō|lt=Needle Memorial Service|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|ja|正月事始め|qq=draft}}
{{translated page|ja|事八日|version=99598614|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{Lunisolar December}} {{Lunisolar February}} [[File:Eitaku Kotoosame.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Sensai Eitaku|ja|鮮斎永濯|qq=draft}} ({{Year|1884}}) *Onko Nenju Gyōji*. Depiction of "Koto-osame" on {{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日|qq=draft}}, with a hanging me-kago (eye basket).]]
'''Kotohachi-ka''' (事八日, "Eighth Day of Matters") or '''Koto Yōka''' is a traditional Japanese annual event. It is also referred to as '''Yōka Sekku''' (八日節供)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkamachi''' (八日待)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkazō''' (八日ぞう)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Koto Hajime''' (事始め)/'''Koto Osame''' (事納め)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''O-koto Hajime''' (お事始め)/'''O-koto Osame''' (お事納め)<ref>『{{ill|Kojien|ja|広辞苑|qq=draft}}』 "Kotohachi-ka"</ref>, '''Yōkabuki''' (八日吹き), '''Yōkagyō''' (八日行), '''Sekku Hajime''' (節供始め)/'''Sekku Osame''' (節供納め), '''O-yakushi-sama''' (お薬師様), or '''Ebisu Kō''' (恵比寿講). In folklore studies, it is collectively referred to as '''Kotohachi-ka'''.<ref name=Nipponica/>
== Overview == Although there are exceptions, it is typically observed either on '''{{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日|qq=draft}}''' or '''{{ill|December 8|ja|12月8日|qq=draft}}''', or on both days in a year. In some regions, one date is called *Koto Hajime* ("Beginning of Matters"), while the other is called *Koto Osame* ("Ending of Matters"). The interpretation of "Koto" (事, matters) varies: in most regions, it refers to '''annual festivals or agricultural work''', making February 8 the "beginning" and December 8 the "end." However, in parts of '''{{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft}}''', "Koto" is understood as '''New Year celebrations''', meaning that '''December marks the beginning and February the end'''.<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf Tochigi Prefectural Customs and Language Collection] {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|qq=draft}}, retrieved January 20, 2021.</ref>.
The content of the event varies significantly by region, and it has gradually declined since the postwar period.
== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|qq=draft}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher={{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|qq=draft}}|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - {{ill|Namioka, Aomori|ja|浪岡町|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hiraga, Hirosaki|ja|平賀町|qq=draft}} in '''{{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft}}''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|qq=draft}} in '''{{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|qq=draft}}''' - {{ill|Sukagawa, Fukushima|ja|須賀川市|qq=draft}} in '''{{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|qq=draft}}'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher={{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学|qq=draft}} |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''{{ill|Warabi, Saitama|ja|蕨市|qq=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Toda, Saitama|ja|戸田市|qq=draft}}''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Urawa-ku, Saitama|ja|浦和区|qq=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Oshima, Tokyo|ja|大島町|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Izu Ōshima|ja|伊豆大島|qq=draft}})''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Sōka, Saitama|ja|草加市|qq=draft}}''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Misato, Saitama|ja|三郷市|qq=draft}}''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Yamatsuri, Fukushima|ja|矢祭町|qq=draft}}''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Hanawa, Fukushima|ja|塙町|qq=draft}}''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - {{ill|Hitotsume-kozō|ja|一つ目小僧|qq=draft}} - {{ill|Mikakaribaba|ja|箕借り婆|qq=draft}} - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神|qq=draft}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯|qq=draft}})
Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ({{ill|Ebisu|ja|えびす|qq=draft}}) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ({{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft}}) * '''Daikokuten''' ({{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|qq=draft}}) * '''Ta no Kami''' ({{ill|Field deity|ja|田の神|qq=draft}}) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|qq=draft}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ({{ill|Hari kuyō|ja|針供養|1=Hari-Kuyō|lt=Needle Memorial Service|qq=draft}}) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft}} * '''February 8''' in {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft}}<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|qq=draft}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.

Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ({{ill|Ebisu|ja|えびす|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebisu|qq=}}) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ({{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medicine Buddha|13=User:Immanuelle/Bhaisajyaguru|qq=}}) * '''Daikokuten''' ({{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikokuten|qq=}}) * '''Ta no Kami''' ({{ill|Field deity|ja|田の神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Field deity|qq=}}) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mountain deity|qq=}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ({{ill|Hari kuyō|ja|針供養|1=Hari-Kuyō|lt=Needle Memorial Service|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hari kuyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hari-Kuyō|qq=}}) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} * '''February 8''' in {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|qq=}}<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|qq=}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Hari kuyō|ja|針供養|1=Hari-Kuyō|lt=Needle Memorial Service|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hari kuyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hari-Kuyō|qq=}} * {{ill|Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|ja|正月事始め|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|事八日|version=99598614|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[sn:Kotohachi-ka]] [[ja:事八日]] [[ar:Kotohachi-ka]] [[Category:Orphaned pages as of May 10, 2025]] [[Category:Orphaned pages as of May 10, 2025]] [[Category:Orphaned pages as of May 10, 2025]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "{{translated page|ja|" to "[[Category:Orphaned translated pages]] {{translated page|ja|"
revision.text:
{{Lunisolar December}} {{Lunisolar February}} [[File:Eitaku Kotoosame.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Sensai Eitaku|ja|鮮斎永濯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sensai Eitaku|qq=}} ({{Year|1884}}) *Onko Nenju Gyōji*. Depiction of "Koto-osame" on {{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8|qq=}}, with a hanging me-kago (eye basket).]]
'''Kotohachi-ka''' (事八日, "Eighth Day of Matters") or '''Koto Yōka''' is a traditional Japanese annual event. It is also referred to as '''Yōka Sekku''' (八日節供)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkamachi''' (八日待)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkazō''' (八日ぞう)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Koto Hajime''' (事始め)/'''Koto Osame''' (事納め)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''O-koto Hajime''' (お事始め)/'''O-koto Osame''' (お事納め)<ref>『{{ill|Kojien|ja|広辞苑|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojien|qq=}}』 "Kotohachi-ka"</ref>, '''Yōkabuki''' (八日吹き), '''Yōkagyō''' (八日行), '''Sekku Hajime''' (節供始め)/'''Sekku Osame''' (節供納め), '''O-yakushi-sama''' (お薬師様), or '''Ebisu Kō''' (恵比寿講). In folklore studies, it is collectively referred to as '''Kotohachi-ka'''.<ref name=Nipponica/>
== Overview == Although there are exceptions, it is typically observed either on '''{{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8|qq=}}''' or '''{{ill|December 8|ja|12月8日|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 8|qq=}}''', or on both days in a year. In some regions, one date is called *Koto Hajime* ("Beginning of Matters"), while the other is called *Koto Osame* ("Ending of Matters"). The interpretation of "Koto" (事, matters) varies: in most regions, it refers to '''annual festivals or agricultural work''', making February 8 the "beginning" and December 8 the "end." However, in parts of '''{{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|qq=}}''', "Koto" is understood as '''New Year celebrations''', meaning that '''December marks the beginning and February the end'''.<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf Tochigi Prefectural Customs and Language Collection] {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tochigi Prefecture|qq=}}, retrieved January 20, 2021.</ref>.
The content of the event varies significantly by region, and it has gradually declined since the postwar period.
== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar March|qq=}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher={{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogakukan|qq=}}|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - {{ill|Namioka, Aomori|ja|浪岡町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namioka, Aomori|qq=}} and {{ill|Hiraga, Hirosaki|ja|平賀町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiraga, Hirosaki|qq=}} in '''{{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Prefecture|qq=}}''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyajuku, Asahi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachi, Kahoku|qq=}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inashita, Murayama|qq=}} in '''{{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamagata Prefecture|qq=}}''' - {{ill|Sukagawa, Fukushima|ja|須賀川市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukagawa, Fukushima|qq=}} in '''{{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushima Prefecture|qq=}}'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher={{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waseda University|qq=}} |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''{{ill|Warabi, Saitama|ja|蕨市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warabi, Saitama|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Toda, Saitama|ja|戸田市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toda, Saitama|qq=}}''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Urawa-ku, Saitama|ja|浦和区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urawa-ku, Saitama|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Oshima, Tokyo|ja|大島町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshima, Tokyo|qq=}} ({{ill|Izu Ōshima|ja|伊豆大島|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izu Ōshima|qq=}})''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Sōka, Saitama|ja|草加市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōka, Saitama|qq=}}''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Misato, Saitama|ja|三郷市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misato, Saitama|qq=}}''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Yamatsuri, Fukushima|ja|矢祭町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatsuri, Fukushima|qq=}}''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Hanawa, Fukushima|ja|塙町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanawa, Fukushima|qq=}}''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - {{ill|Hitotsume-kozō|ja|一つ目小僧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitotsume-kozō|qq=}} - {{ill|Mikakaribaba|ja|箕借り婆|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikakaribaba|qq=}} - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epidemic deities|qq=}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiiragi-iwashi|qq=}})
Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ({{ill|Ebisu|ja|えびす|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebisu|qq=}}) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ({{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medicine Buddha|13=User:Immanuelle/Bhaisajyaguru|qq=}}) * '''Daikokuten''' ({{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikokuten|qq=}}) * '''Ta no Kami''' ({{ill|Field deity|ja|田の神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Field deity|qq=}}) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mountain deity|qq=}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ({{ill|Hari kuyō|ja|針供養|1=Hari-Kuyō|lt=Needle Memorial Service|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hari kuyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hari-Kuyō|qq=}}) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} * '''February 8''' in {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|qq=}}<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|qq=}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Hari kuyō|ja|針供養|1=Hari-Kuyō|lt=Needle Memorial Service|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hari kuyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hari-Kuyō|qq=}} * {{ill|Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|ja|正月事始め|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|qq=}}
[[Category:Orphaned translated pages]] {{translated page|ja|事八日|version=99598614|comment=Imported full ja history}}

are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ({{ill|Hari kuyō|ja|針供養|1=Hari-Kuyō|lt=Needle Memorial Service|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hari kuyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hari-Kuyō|qq=}}) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} * '''February 8''' in {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|qq=}}<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|qq=}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.
