[[sn:Kotohachi-ka]] [[ja:事八日]] [[ar:Kotohachi-ka]]

{{Lunisolar December}} {{Lunisolar February}} [[File:Eitaku Kotoosame.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Sensai Eitaku|ja|鮮斎永濯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sensai Eitaku|qq=}} ({{Year|1884}}) *Onko Nenju Gyōji*. Depiction of "Koto-osame" on {{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8|qq=}}, with a hanging me-kago (eye basket).]]
'''Kotohachi-ka''' (事八日, "Eighth Day of Matters") or '''Koto Yōka''' is a traditional Japanese annual event. It is also referred to as '''Yōka Sekku''' (八日節供)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkamachi''' (八日待)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkazō''' (八日ぞう)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Koto Hajime''' (事始め)/'''Koto Osame''' (事納め)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''O-koto Hajime''' (お事始め)/'''O-koto Osame''' (お事納め)<ref>『{{ill|Kojien|ja|広辞苑|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojien|qq=}}』 "Kotohachi-ka"</ref>, '''Yōkabuki''' (八日吹き), '''Yōkagyō''' (八日行), '''Sekku Hajime''' (節供始め)/'''Sekku Osame''' (節供納め), '''O-yakushi-sama''' (お薬師様), or '''Ebisu Kō''' (恵比寿講). In folklore studies, it is collectively referred to as '''Kotohachi-ka'''.<ref name=Nipponica/>
== Overview == Although there are exceptions, it is typically observed either on '''{{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8|qq=}}''' or '''{{ill|December 8|ja|12月8日|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 8|qq=}}''', or on both days in a year. In some regions, one date is called *Koto Hajime* ("Beginning of Matters"), while the other is called *Koto Osame* ("Ending of Matters"). The interpretation of "Koto" (事, matters) varies: in most regions, it refers to '''annual festivals or agricultural work''', making February 8 the "beginning" and December 8 the "end." However, in parts of '''{{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|qq=}}''', "Koto" is understood as '''New Year celebrations''', meaning that '''December marks the beginning and February the end'''.<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf Tochigi Prefectural Customs and Language Collection] {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tochigi Prefecture|qq=}}, retrieved January 20, 2021.</ref>.
The content of the event varies significantly by region, and it has gradually declined since the postwar period.
== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar March|qq=}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher={{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogakukan|qq=}}|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - {{ill|Namioka, Aomori|ja|浪岡町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namioka, Aomori|qq=}} and {{ill|Hiraga, Hirosaki|ja|平賀町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiraga, Hirosaki|qq=}} in '''{{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Prefecture|qq=}}''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyajuku, Asahi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachi, Kahoku|qq=}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inashita, Murayama|qq=}} in '''{{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamagata Prefecture|qq=}}''' - {{ill|Sukagawa, Fukushima|ja|須賀川市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukagawa, Fukushima|qq=}} in '''{{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushima Prefecture|qq=}}'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher={{ill|Waseda University|ja|早稲田大学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waseda University|qq=}} |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''{{ill|Warabi, Saitama|ja|蕨市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warabi, Saitama|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Toda, Saitama|ja|戸田市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toda, Saitama|qq=}}''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Urawa-ku, Saitama|ja|浦和区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urawa-ku, Saitama|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Oshima, Tokyo|ja|大島町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshima, Tokyo|qq=}} ({{ill|Izu Ōshima|ja|伊豆大島|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izu Ōshima|qq=}})''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Sōka, Saitama|ja|草加市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōka, Saitama|qq=}}''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Misato, Saitama|ja|三郷市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misato, Saitama|qq=}}''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Yamatsuri, Fukushima|ja|矢祭町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatsuri, Fukushima|qq=}}''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''{{ill|Hanawa, Fukushima|ja|塙町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanawa, Fukushima|qq=}}''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - {{ill|Hitotsume-kozō|ja|一つ目小僧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitotsume-kozō|qq=}} - {{ill|Mikakaribaba|ja|箕借り婆|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikakaribaba|qq=}} - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epidemic deities|qq=}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiiragi-iwashi|qq=}})
Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ({{ill|Ebisu|ja|えびす|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebisu|qq=}}) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ({{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medicine Buddha|13=User:Immanuelle/Bhaisajyaguru|qq=}}) * '''Daikokuten''' ({{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikokuten|qq=}}) * '''Ta no Kami''' ({{ill|Field deity|ja|田の神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Field deity|qq=}}) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mountain deity|qq=}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ({{ill|Hari kuyō|ja|針供養|1=Hari-Kuyō|lt=Needle Memorial Service|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hari kuyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hari-Kuyō|qq=}}) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} * '''February 8''' in {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|qq=}}<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|qq=}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Hari kuyō|ja|針供養|1=Hari-Kuyō|lt=Needle Memorial Service|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hari kuyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hari-Kuyō|qq=}} * {{ill|Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|ja|正月事始め|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|qq=}}

Depiction of "Koto-osame" on [[:en:February 8|February 8]], with a hanging me-kago (eye basket).]]
'''Kotohachi-ka''' (事八日, "Eighth Day of Matters") or '''Koto Yōka''' is a traditional Japanese annual event. It is also referred to as '''Yōka Sekku''' (八日節供)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkamachi''' (八日待)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkazō''' (八日ぞう)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Koto Hajime''' (事始め)/'''Koto Osame''' (事納め)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''O-koto Hajime''' (お事始め)/'''O-koto Osame''' (お事納め)<ref>『[[:en:Kōjien|Kojien]]』 "Kotohachi-ka"</ref>, '''Yōkabuki''' (八日吹き), '''Yōkagyō''' (八日行), '''Sekku Hajime''' (節供始め)/'''Sekku Osame''' (節供納め), '''O-yakushi-sama''' (お薬師様), or '''Ebisu Kō''' (恵比寿講). In folklore studies, it is collectively referred to as '''Kotohachi-ka'''.<ref name=Nipponica/>
== Overview == Although there are exceptions, it is typically observed either on '''[[:en:February 8|February 8]]''' or '''[[:en:December 8|December 8]]''', or on both days in a year. In some regions, one date is called *Koto Hajime* ("Beginning of Matters"), while the other is called *Koto Osame* ("Ending of Matters"). The interpretation of "Koto" (事, matters) varies: in most regions, it refers to '''annual festivals or agricultural work''', making February 8 the "beginning" and December 8 the "end." However, in parts of '''[[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]''', "Koto" is understood as '''New Year celebrations''', meaning that '''December marks the beginning and February the end'''.<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf Tochigi Prefectural Customs and Language Collection] [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], retrieved January 20, 2021.</ref>.
The content of the event varies significantly by region, and it has gradually declined since the postwar period.
== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar March|qq=}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher=[[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]]|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - [[:en:Namioka, Aomori|Namioka, Aomori]] and [[:en:Hiraka, Aomori|Hiraga, Hirosaki]] in '''[[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]]''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyajuku, Asahi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachi, Kahoku|qq=}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inashita, Murayama|qq=}} in '''[[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]''' - [[:en:Sukagawa, Fukushima|Sukagawa, Fukushima]] in '''[[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher=[[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''[[:en:Warabi, Saitama|Warabi, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Toda, Saitama|Toda, Saitama]]''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Urawa-ku, Saitama|Urawa-ku, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Ōshima, Tokyo|Oshima, Tokyo]] ([[:en:Izu Ōshima|Izu Ōshima]])''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Sōka|Sōka, Saitama]]''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Misato, Saitama (city)|Misato, Saitama]]''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Yamatsuri, Fukushima|Yamatsuri, Fukushima]]''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Hanawa, Fukushima|Hanawa, Fukushima]]''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - [[:en:Hitotsume-kozō|Hitotsume-kozō]] - [[:en:Mikaribaba|Mikakaribaba]] - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epidemic deities|qq=}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiiragi-iwashi|qq=}})
Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ([[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]]) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ([[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]) * '''Daikokuten''' ([[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]) * '''Ta no Kami''' ([[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Field deity]]) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mountain deity|qq=}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ([[:en:Hari-Kuyō|Needle Memorial Service]]) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} * '''February 8''' in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|qq=}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.

In folklore studies, it is collectively referred to as '''Kotohachi-ka'''.<ref name=Nipponica/>

In some regions, one date is called *Koto Hajime* ("Beginning of Matters"), while the other is called *Koto Osame* ("Ending of Matters"). The interpretation of "Koto" (事, matters) varies: in most regions, it refers to '''annual festivals or agricultural work''', making February 8 the "beginning" and December 8 the "end." However, in parts of '''[[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]''', "Koto" is understood as '''New Year celebrations''', meaning that '''December marks the beginning and February the end'''.<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf Tochigi Prefectural Customs and Language Collection] [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], retrieved January 20, 2021.</ref>.
The content of the event varies significantly by region, and it has gradually declined since the postwar period.
== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar March|qq=}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher=[[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]]|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - [[:en:Namioka, Aomori|Namioka, Aomori]] and [[:en:Hiraka, Aomori|Hiraga, Hirosaki]] in '''[[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]]''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyajuku, Asahi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachi, Kahoku|qq=}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inashita, Murayama|qq=}} in '''[[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]''' - [[:en:Sukagawa, Fukushima|Sukagawa, Fukushima]] in '''[[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher=[[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''[[:en:Warabi, Saitama|Warabi, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Toda, Saitama|Toda, Saitama]]''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Urawa-ku, Saitama|Urawa-ku, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Ōshima, Tokyo|Oshima, Tokyo]] ([[:en:Izu Ōshima|Izu Ōshima]])''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Sōka|Sōka, Saitama]]''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Misato, Saitama (city)|Misato, Saitama]]''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Yamatsuri, Fukushima|Yamatsuri, Fukushima]]''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Hanawa, Fukushima|Hanawa, Fukushima]]''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - [[:en:Hitotsume-kozō|Hitotsume-kozō]] - [[:en:Mikaribaba|Mikakaribaba]] - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epidemic deities|qq=}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiiragi-iwashi|qq=}})
Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ([[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]]) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ([[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]) * '''Daikokuten''' ([[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]) * '''Ta no Kami''' ([[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Field deity]]) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mountain deity|qq=}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ([[:en:Hari-Kuyō|Needle Memorial Service]]) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} * '''February 8''' in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|qq=}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Hari-Kuyō|Needle Memorial Service]] * {{ill|Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|ja|正月事始め|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|qq=}}

[[sn:Kotohachi-ka]] [[ja:事八日]] [[ar:Kotohachi-ka]] [[Category:Annual events in Japan]]

{{Lunisolar December}} {{Lunisolar February}} [[File:Eitaku Kotoosame.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Sensai Eitaku|ja|鮮斎永濯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sensai Eitaku|qq=}} ({{Year|1884}}) *Onko Nenju Gyōji*. Depiction of "Koto-osame" on [[:en:February 8|February 8]], with a hanging me-kago (eye basket).]]
'''Kotohachi-ka''' (事八日, "Eighth Day of Matters") or '''Koto Yōka''' is a traditional Japanese annual event. It is also referred to as '''Yōka Sekku''' (八日節供)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkamachi''' (八日待)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkazō''' (八日ぞう)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Koto Hajime''' (事始め)/'''Koto Osame''' (事納め)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''O-koto Hajime''' (お事始め)/'''O-koto Osame''' (お事納め)<ref>『[[:en:Kōjien|Kojien]]』 "Kotohachi-ka"</ref>, '''Yōkabuki''' (八日吹き), '''Yōkagyō''' (八日行), '''Sekku Hajime''' (節供始め)/'''Sekku Osame''' (節供納め), '''O-yakushi-sama''' (お薬師様), or '''Ebisu Kō''' (恵比寿講). In folklore studies, it is collectively referred to as '''Kotohachi-ka'''.<ref name=Nipponica/>
== Overview == Although there are exceptions, it is typically observed either on '''[[:en:February 8|February 8]]''' or '''[[:en:December 8|December 8]]''', or on both days in a year. In some regions, one date is called *Koto Hajime* ("Beginning of Matters"), while the other is called *Koto Osame* ("Ending of Matters"). The interpretation of "Koto" (事, matters) varies: in most regions, it refers to '''annual festivals or agricultural work''', making February 8 the "beginning" and December 8 the "end." However, in parts of '''[[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]''', "Koto" is understood as '''New Year celebrations''', meaning that '''December marks the beginning and February the end'''.<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf Tochigi Prefectural Customs and Language Collection] [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], retrieved January 20, 2021.</ref>.
The content of the event varies significantly by region, and it has gradually declined since the postwar period.
== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar March|qq=}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher=[[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]]|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - [[:en:Namioka, Aomori|Namioka, Aomori]] and [[:en:Hiraka, Aomori|Hiraga, Hirosaki]] in '''[[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]]''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyajuku, Asahi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachi, Kahoku|qq=}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inashita, Murayama|qq=}} in '''[[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]''' - [[:en:Sukagawa, Fukushima|Sukagawa, Fukushima]] in '''[[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher=[[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''[[:en:Warabi, Saitama|Warabi, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Toda, Saitama|Toda, Saitama]]''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Urawa-ku, Saitama|Urawa-ku, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Ōshima, Tokyo|Oshima, Tokyo]] ([[:en:Izu Ōshima|Izu Ōshima]])''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Sōka|Sōka, Saitama]]''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Misato, Saitama (city)|Misato, Saitama]]''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Yamatsuri, Fukushima|Yamatsuri, Fukushima]]''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Hanawa, Fukushima|Hanawa, Fukushima]]''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - [[:en:Hitotsume-kozō|Hitotsume-kozō]] - [[:en:Mikaribaba|Mikakaribaba]] - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epidemic deities|qq=}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiiragi-iwashi|qq=}})
Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ([[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]]) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ([[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]) * '''Daikokuten''' ([[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]) * '''Ta no Kami''' ([[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Field deity]]) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mountain deity|qq=}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ([[:en:Hari-Kuyō|Needle Memorial Service]]) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} * '''February 8''' in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|qq=}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.

Depiction of "Koto-osame" on [[:en:February 8|February 8]], with a hanging me-kago (eye basket).]]
'''Kotohachi-ka''' (事八日, "Eighth Day of Matters") or '''Koto Yōka''' is a traditional Japanese annual event. It is also referred to as '''Yōka Sekku''' (八日節供)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkamachi''' (八日待)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkazō''' (八日ぞう)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Koto Hajime''' (事始め)/'''Koto Osame''' (事納め)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''O-koto Hajime''' (お事始め)/'''O-koto Osame''' (お事納め)<ref>『[[:en:Kōjien|Kojien]]』 "Kotohachi-ka"</ref>, '''Yōkabuki''' (八日吹き), '''Yōkagyō''' (八日行), '''Sekku Hajime''' (節供始め)/'''Sekku Osame''' (節供納め), '''O-yakushi-sama''' (お薬師様), or '''Ebisu Kō''' (恵比寿講). In folklore studies, it is collectively referred to as '''Kotohachi-ka'''.<ref name=Nipponica/>
== Overview == Although there are exceptions, it is typically observed either on '''[[:en:February 8|February 8]]''' or '''[[:en:December 8|December 8]]''', or on both days in a year. In some regions, one date is called *Koto Hajime* ("Beginning of Matters"), while the other is called *Koto Osame* ("Ending of Matters"). The interpretation of "Koto" (事, matters) varies: in most regions, it refers to '''annual festivals or agricultural work''', making February 8 the "beginning" and December 8 the "end." However, in parts of '''[[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]''', "Koto" is understood as '''New Year celebrations''', meaning that '''December marks the beginning and February the end'''.<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf Tochigi Prefectural Customs and Language Collection] [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], retrieved January 20, 2021.</ref>.
The content of the event varies significantly by region, and it has gradually declined since the postwar period.
== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar March|qq=}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher=[[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]]|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - [[:en:Namioka, Aomori|Namioka, Aomori]] and [[:en:Hiraka, Aomori|Hiraga, Hirosaki]] in '''[[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]]''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyajuku, Asahi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachi, Kahoku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inashita, Murayama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in '''[[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]''' - [[:en:Sukagawa, Fukushima|Sukagawa, Fukushima]] in '''[[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher=[[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''[[:en:Warabi, Saitama|Warabi, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Toda, Saitama|Toda, Saitama]]''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Urawa-ku, Saitama|Urawa-ku, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Ōshima, Tokyo|Oshima, Tokyo]] ([[:en:Izu Ōshima|Izu Ōshima]])''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Sōka|Sōka, Saitama]]''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Misato, Saitama (city)|Misato, Saitama]]''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Yamatsuri, Fukushima|Yamatsuri, Fukushima]]''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Hanawa, Fukushima|Hanawa, Fukushima]]''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - [[:en:Hitotsume-kozō|Hitotsume-kozō]] - [[:en:Mikaribaba|Mikakaribaba]] - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epidemic deities|qq=}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiiragi-iwashi|qq=}})
Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ([[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]]) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ([[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]) * '''Daikokuten''' ([[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]) * '''Ta no Kami''' ([[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Field deity]]) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mountain deity|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ([[:en:Hari-Kuyō|Needle Memorial Service]]) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西}} * '''February 8''' in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Hari-Kuyō|Needle Memorial Service]] * {{ill|Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|ja|正月事始め|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|qq=}}

Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ([[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]]) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ([[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]) * '''Daikokuten''' ([[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]) * '''Ta no Kami''' ([[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Field deity]]) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mountain deity|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ([[:en:Hari-Kuyō|Needle Memorial Service]]) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西}} * '''February 8''' in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Hari-Kuyō|Needle Memorial Service]] * {{ill|Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|ja|正月事始め|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|qq=}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q21653060]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q21653060]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Lunisolar December}} {{Lunisolar February}} [[File:Eitaku Kotoosame.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Sensai Eitaku|ja|鮮斎永濯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sensai Eitaku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 小林永濯}} ({{Year|1884}}) *Onko Nenju Gyōji*. Depiction of "Koto-osame" on [[:en:February 8|February 8]], with a hanging me-kago (eye basket).]]
'''Kotohachi-ka''' (事八日, "Eighth Day of Matters") or '''Koto Yōka''' is a traditional Japanese annual event. It is also referred to as '''Yōka Sekku''' (八日節供)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkamachi''' (八日待)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkazō''' (八日ぞう)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Koto Hajime''' (事始め)/'''Koto Osame''' (事納め)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''O-koto Hajime''' (お事始め)/'''O-koto Osame''' (お事納め)<ref>『[[:en:Kōjien|Kojien]]』 "Kotohachi-ka"</ref>, '''Yōkabuki''' (八日吹き), '''Yōkagyō''' (八日行), '''Sekku Hajime''' (節供始め)/'''Sekku Osame''' (節供納め), '''O-yakushi-sama''' (お薬師様), or '''Ebisu Kō''' (恵比寿講). In folklore studies, it is collectively referred to as '''Kotohachi-ka'''.<ref name=Nipponica/>
== Overview == Although there are exceptions, it is typically observed either on '''[[:en:February 8|February 8]]''' or '''[[:en:December 8|December 8]]''', or on both days in a year. In some regions, one date is called *Koto Hajime* ("Beginning of Matters"), while the other is called *Koto Osame* ("Ending of Matters"). The interpretation of "Koto" (事, matters) varies: in most regions, it refers to '''annual festivals or agricultural work''', making February 8 the "beginning" and December 8 the "end." However, in parts of '''[[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]''', "Koto" is understood as '''New Year celebrations''', meaning that '''December marks the beginning and February the end'''.<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf Tochigi Prefectural Customs and Language Collection] [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], retrieved January 20, 2021.</ref>.
The content of the event varies significantly by region, and it has gradually declined since the postwar period.
== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar March|qq=}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher=[[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]]|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - [[:en:Namioka, Aomori|Namioka, Aomori]] and [[:en:Hiraka, Aomori|Hiraga, Hirosaki]] in '''[[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]]''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyajuku, Asahi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachi, Kahoku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inashita, Murayama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in '''[[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]''' - [[:en:Sukagawa, Fukushima|Sukagawa, Fukushima]] in '''[[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher=[[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''[[:en:Warabi, Saitama|Warabi, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Toda, Saitama|Toda, Saitama]]''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Urawa-ku, Saitama|Urawa-ku, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Ōshima, Tokyo|Oshima, Tokyo]] ([[:en:Izu Ōshima|Izu Ōshima]])''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Sōka|Sōka, Saitama]]''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Misato, Saitama (city)|Misato, Saitama]]''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Yamatsuri, Fukushima|Yamatsuri, Fukushima]]''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Hanawa, Fukushima|Hanawa, Fukushima]]''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - [[:en:Hitotsume-kozō|Hitotsume-kozō]] - [[:en:Mikaribaba|Mikakaribaba]] - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epidemic deities|qq=}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiiragi-iwashi|qq=}})
Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ([[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]]) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ([[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]) * '''Daikokuten''' ([[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]) * '''Ta no Kami''' ([[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Field deity]]) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mountain deity|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ([[:en:Hari-Kuyō|Needle Memorial Service]]) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西}} * '''February 8''' in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Hari-Kuyō|Needle Memorial Service]] * {{ill|Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|ja|正月事始め|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|qq=}}

== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar March|qq=|1=3rd month|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838927}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher=[[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]]|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - [[:en:Namioka, Aomori|Namioka, Aomori]] and [[:en:Hiraka, Aomori|Hiraga, Hirosaki]] in '''[[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]]''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyajuku, Asahi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachi, Kahoku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inashita, Murayama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in '''[[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]''' - [[:en:Sukagawa, Fukushima|Sukagawa, Fukushima]] in '''[[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher=[[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''[[:en:Warabi, Saitama|Warabi, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Toda, Saitama|Toda, Saitama]]''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Urawa-ku, Saitama|Urawa-ku, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Ōshima, Tokyo|Oshima, Tokyo]] ([[:en:Izu Ōshima|Izu Ōshima]])''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Sōka|Sōka, Saitama]]''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Misato, Saitama (city)|Misato, Saitama]]''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Yamatsuri, Fukushima|Yamatsuri, Fukushima]]''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Hanawa, Fukushima|Hanawa, Fukushima]]''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - [[:en:Hitotsume-kozō|Hitotsume-kozō]] - [[:en:Mikaribaba|Mikakaribaba]] - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epidemic deities|qq=|1=Yakubyōgami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578909}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiiragi-iwashi|qq=|1=hiiragi iwashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533638}})
Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ([[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]]) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ([[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]) * '''Daikokuten''' ([[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]) * '''Ta no Kami''' ([[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Field deity]]) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mountain deity|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Yama-no-Kami|WD=Q839254}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ([[:en:Hari-Kuyō|Needle Memorial Service]]) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}} * '''February 8''' in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Hari-Kuyō|Needle Memorial Service]] * {{ill|Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|ja|正月事始め|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|qq=|1=Shogatsu Jishime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545487}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q21653060]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q21653060]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Lunisolar December}} {{Lunisolar February}} [[File:Eitaku Kotoosame.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Sensai Eitaku|ja|鮮斎永濯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sensai Eitaku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 小林永濯|1=Kobayashi Eitaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2784105}} ({{Year|1884}}) *Onko Nenju Gyōji*. Depiction of "Koto-osame" on [[:en:February 8|February 8]], with a hanging me-kago (eye basket).]]
'''Kotohachi-ka''' (事八日, "Eighth Day of Matters") or '''Koto Yōka''' is a traditional Japanese annual event. It is also referred to as '''Yōka Sekku''' (八日節供)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkamachi''' (八日待)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkazō''' (八日ぞう)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Koto Hajime''' (事始め)/'''Koto Osame''' (事納め)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''O-koto Hajime''' (お事始め)/'''O-koto Osame''' (お事納め)<ref>『[[:en:Kōjien|Kojien]]』 "Kotohachi-ka"</ref>, '''Yōkabuki''' (八日吹き), '''Yōkagyō''' (八日行), '''Sekku Hajime''' (節供始め)/'''Sekku Osame''' (節供納め), '''O-yakushi-sama''' (お薬師様), or '''Ebisu Kō''' (恵比寿講). In folklore studies, it is collectively referred to as '''Kotohachi-ka'''.<ref name=Nipponica/>
== Overview == Although there are exceptions, it is typically observed either on '''[[:en:February 8|February 8]]''' or '''[[:en:December 8|December 8]]''', or on both days in a year. In some regions, one date is called *Koto Hajime* ("Beginning of Matters"), while the other is called *Koto Osame* ("Ending of Matters"). The interpretation of "Koto" (事, matters) varies: in most regions, it refers to '''annual festivals or agricultural work''', making February 8 the "beginning" and December 8 the "end." However, in parts of '''[[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]''', "Koto" is understood as '''New Year celebrations''', meaning that '''December marks the beginning and February the end'''.<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf Tochigi Prefectural Customs and Language Collection] [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], retrieved January 20, 2021.</ref>.
The content of the event varies significantly by region, and it has gradually declined since the postwar period.
== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar March|qq=|1=3rd month|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838927}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher=[[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]]|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - [[:en:Namioka, Aomori|Namioka, Aomori]] and [[:en:Hiraka, Aomori|Hiraga, Hirosaki]] in '''[[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]]''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyajuku, Asahi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachi, Kahoku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inashita, Murayama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in '''[[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]''' - [[:en:Sukagawa, Fukushima|Sukagawa, Fukushima]] in '''[[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher=[[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''[[:en:Warabi, Saitama|Warabi, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Toda, Saitama|Toda, Saitama]]''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Urawa-ku, Saitama|Urawa-ku, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Ōshima, Tokyo|Oshima, Tokyo]] ([[:en:Izu Ōshima|Izu Ōshima]])''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Sōka|Sōka, Saitama]]''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Misato, Saitama (city)|Misato, Saitama]]''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Yamatsuri, Fukushima|Yamatsuri, Fukushima]]''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Hanawa, Fukushima|Hanawa, Fukushima]]''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - [[:en:Hitotsume-kozō|Hitotsume-kozō]] - [[:en:Mikaribaba|Mikakaribaba]] - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epidemic deities|qq=|1=Yakubyōgami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578909}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiiragi-iwashi|qq=|1=hiiragi iwashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533638}})
Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ([[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]]) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ([[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]) * '''Daikokuten''' ([[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]) * '''Ta no Kami''' ([[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Field deity]]) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mountain deity|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Yama-no-Kami|WD=Q839254}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ([[:en:Hari-Kuyō|Needle Memorial Service]]) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}} * '''February 8''' in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Hari-Kuyō|Needle Memorial Service]] * {{ill|Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|ja|正月事始め|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|qq=|1=Shogatsu Jishime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545487}}

Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ([[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]]) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ([[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]) * '''Daikokuten''' ([[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]) * '''Ta no Kami''' ([[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Field deity]]) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mountain deity|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Yama-no-Kami|WD=Q839254}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ([[:en:Hari-Kuyō|Needle Memorial Service]]) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}} * '''February 8''' in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.

[[ja:事八日]] [[ar:Kotohachi-ka]] [[Category:Annual events in Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] {{wikidata link|Q21653060}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:事八日]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q21653060]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q21653060]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Lunisolar December}} {{Lunisolar February}} [[File:Eitaku Kotoosame.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Sensai Eitaku|ja|鮮斎永濯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sensai Eitaku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 小林永濯|1=Kobayashi Eitaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2784105}} ({{Year|1884}}) *Onko Nenju Gyōji*. Depiction of "Koto-osame" on [[:en:February 8|February 8]], with a hanging me-kago (eye basket).]]
'''Kotohachi-ka''' (事八日, "Eighth Day of Matters") or '''Koto Yōka''' is a traditional Japanese annual event. It is also referred to as '''Yōka Sekku''' (八日節供)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkamachi''' (八日待)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkazō''' (八日ぞう)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Koto Hajime''' (事始め)/'''Koto Osame''' (事納め)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''O-koto Hajime''' (お事始め)/'''O-koto Osame''' (お事納め)<ref>『[[:en:Kōjien|Kojien]]』 "Kotohachi-ka"</ref>, '''Yōkabuki''' (八日吹き), '''Yōkagyō''' (八日行), '''Sekku Hajime''' (節供始め)/'''Sekku Osame''' (節供納め), '''O-yakushi-sama''' (お薬師様), or '''Ebisu Kō''' (恵比寿講). In folklore studies, it is collectively referred to as '''Kotohachi-ka'''.<ref name=Nipponica/>
== Overview == Although there are exceptions, it is typically observed either on '''[[:en:February 8|February 8]]''' or '''[[:en:December 8|December 8]]''', or on both days in a year. In some regions, one date is called *Koto Hajime* ("Beginning of Matters"), while the other is called *Koto Osame* ("Ending of Matters"). The interpretation of "Koto" (事, matters) varies: in most regions, it refers to '''annual festivals or agricultural work''', making February 8 the "beginning" and December 8 the "end." However, in parts of '''[[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]''', "Koto" is understood as '''New Year celebrations''', meaning that '''December marks the beginning and February the end'''.<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf Tochigi Prefectural Customs and Language Collection] [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], retrieved January 20, 2021.</ref>.
The content of the event varies significantly by region, and it has gradually declined since the postwar period.
== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar March|qq=|1=3rd month|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838927}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher=[[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]]|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - [[:en:Namioka, Aomori|Namioka, Aomori]] and [[:en:Hiraka, Aomori|Hiraga, Hirosaki]] in '''[[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]]''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyajuku, Asahi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachi, Kahoku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inashita, Murayama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in '''[[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]''' - [[:en:Sukagawa, Fukushima|Sukagawa, Fukushima]] in '''[[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher=[[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''[[:en:Warabi, Saitama|Warabi, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Toda, Saitama|Toda, Saitama]]''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Urawa-ku, Saitama|Urawa-ku, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Ōshima, Tokyo|Oshima, Tokyo]] ([[:en:Izu Ōshima|Izu Ōshima]])''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Sōka|Sōka, Saitama]]''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Misato, Saitama (city)|Misato, Saitama]]''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Yamatsuri, Fukushima|Yamatsuri, Fukushima]]''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Hanawa, Fukushima|Hanawa, Fukushima]]''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - [[:en:Hitotsume-kozō|Hitotsume-kozō]] - [[:en:Mikaribaba|Mikakaribaba]] - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epidemic deities|qq=|1=Yakubyōgami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578909}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiiragi-iwashi|qq=|1=hiiragi iwashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533638}})
Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ([[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]]) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ([[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]) * '''Daikokuten''' ([[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]) * '''Ta no Kami''' ([[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Field deity]]) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mountain deity|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Yama-no-Kami|WD=Q839254}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ([[:en:Hari-Kuyō|Needle Memorial Service]]) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}} * '''February 8''' in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.

'''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar March|qq=|1=3rd month|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838927}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher=[[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]]|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - [[:en:Namioka, Aomori|Namioka, Aomori]] and [[:en:Hiraka, Aomori|Hiraga, Hirosaki]] in '''[[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]]''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyajuku, Asahi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachi, Kahoku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inashita, Murayama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in '''[[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]''' - [[:en:Sukagawa, Fukushima|Sukagawa, Fukushima]] in '''[[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher=[[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''[[:en:Warabi, Saitama|Warabi, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Toda, Saitama|Toda, Saitama]]''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Urawa-ku, Saitama|Urawa-ku, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Ōshima, Tokyo|Oshima, Tokyo]] ([[:en:Izu Ōshima|Izu Ōshima]])''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Sōka|Sōka, Saitama]]''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Misato, Saitama (city)|Misato, Saitama]]''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Yamatsuri, Fukushima|Yamatsuri, Fukushima]]''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Hanawa, Fukushima|Hanawa, Fukushima]]''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. Some include: - [[:en:Hitotsume-kozō|Hitotsume-kozō]] - [[:en:Mikaribaba|Mikakaribaba]] - {{ill|Epidemic deities|ja|疫病神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epidemic deities|qq=|1=Yakubyōgami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578909}} * '''Daimanako''' (ダイマナコ)
To ward off these spirits, '''protective charms''' are hung at the entrance of homes, including: * '''Me-kago''' (目籠, eye baskets) * '''Dried porcupine fish''' * '''Beehives''' * '''Mugwort''' * '''Japanese pepper''' * '''Chili peppers''' * '''Garlic''' * '''Holly leaves with sardine heads''' ({{ill|Hiiragi-iwashi|ja|柊鰯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiiragi-iwashi|qq=|1=hiiragi iwashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533638}})
Conversely, in some regions, benevolent deities such as: * '''Ebisu''' ([[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]]) * '''Yakushi Nyorai''' ([[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]) * '''Daikokuten''' ([[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]) * '''Ta no Kami''' ([[:en:Ta-no-Kami|Field deity]]) * '''Yama no Kami''' ({{ill|Mountain deity|ja|山の神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mountain deity|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Yama-no-Kami|WD=Q839254}})
are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.
In '''Southern Tochigi and Southwest Ibaraki''', '''"Sasa-gami-sama" (笹神様)''' made of bundled bamboo grass is displayed in yards. This practice has been designated a '''National Cultural Property of Northern Kantō'''<ref name="tochigi" />.
Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ([[:en:Hari-Kuyō|Needle Memorial Service]]) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}} * '''February 8''' in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:事八日]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q21653060]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q21653060]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Lunisolar December}} {{Lunisolar February}} [[File:Eitaku Kotoosame.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Sensai Eitaku|ja|鮮斎永濯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sensai Eitaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 小林永濯|1=Kobayashi Eitaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2784105}} ({{Year|1884}}) *Onko Nenju Gyōji*. Depiction of "Koto-osame" on [[:en:February 8|February 8]], with a hanging me-kago (eye basket).]]
'''Kotohachi-ka''' (事八日, "Eighth Day of Matters") or '''Koto Yōka''' is a traditional Japanese annual event. It is also referred to as '''Yōka Sekku''' (八日節供)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkamachi''' (八日待)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Yōkazō''' (八日ぞう)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''Koto Hajime''' (事始め)/'''Koto Osame''' (事納め)<ref name=Nipponica/>, '''O-koto Hajime''' (お事始め)/'''O-koto Osame''' (お事納め)<ref>『[[:en:Kōjien|Kojien]]』 "Kotohachi-ka"</ref>, '''Yōkabuki''' (八日吹き), '''Yōkagyō''' (八日行), '''Sekku Hajime''' (節供始め)/'''Sekku Osame''' (節供納め), '''O-yakushi-sama''' (お薬師様), or '''Ebisu Kō''' (恵比寿講). In folklore studies, it is collectively referred to as '''Kotohachi-ka'''.<ref name=Nipponica/>
== Overview == Although there are exceptions, it is typically observed either on '''[[:en:February 8|February 8]]''' or '''[[:en:December 8|December 8]]''', or on both days in a year. In some regions, one date is called *Koto Hajime* ("Beginning of Matters"), while the other is called *Koto Osame* ("Ending of Matters"). The interpretation of "Koto" (事, matters) varies: in most regions, it refers to '''annual festivals or agricultural work''', making February 8 the "beginning" and December 8 the "end." However, in parts of '''[[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]''', "Koto" is understood as '''New Year celebrations''', meaning that '''December marks the beginning and February the end'''.<ref name=NMJ/><ref name="tochigi">[http://www.pref.tochigi.lg.jp/m06/shinkou/documents/zentoosi.pdf Tochigi Prefectural Customs and Language Collection] [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], retrieved January 20, 2021.</ref>.
The content of the event varies significantly by region, and it has gradually declined since the postwar period.
== Date == In some regions, only one of the two dates is observed. '''Eastern Japan (Chūbu region and beyond)''' generally observes both, whereas '''Western Japan (from Hokuriku westward)''' primarily observes the December event. In particular, in the '''Kinki and Chūgoku regions''', the event was absorbed into '''Spring Matters (春事, Harugoto)''' around {{ill|Lunisolar March|ja|3月 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar March|1=3rd month|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838927}} and is typically only observed in December.<ref name=NMJ>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本民俗事典|chapter=事八日|year=1972|author=田中宣一|authorlink=田中宣一}}</ref><ref name=Nipponica>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本大百科全書|chapter=事八日|publisher=[[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]]|author=小川直之|authorlink=小川直之}}</ref>.
Originally, the event followed the '''Chinese lunisolar calendar''', and even as late as the 1970s, it was still observed according to the lunar calendar in some areas, such as: - [[:en:Namioka, Aomori|Namioka, Aomori]] and [[:en:Hiraka, Aomori|Hiraga, Hirosaki]] in '''[[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]]''' - {{ill|Miyajuku, Asahi|ja|宮宿 (朝日町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyajuku, Asahi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Yachi, Kahoku|ja|谷地 (河北町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachi, Kahoku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Inashita, Murayama|ja|稲下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inashita, Murayama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in '''[[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]''' - [[:en:Sukagawa, Fukushima|Sukagawa, Fukushima]] in '''[[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]'''<ref name=Cho>{{cite thesis|和書|author=曹圭憲 |title=「コト八日」の祭祀論的研究 : 神去来思想による稲作一元論・祖霊一元論を超えて |publisher=[[:en:Waseda University|Waseda University]] |series=博士(人間科学) 甲第2473号 |year=2007 |naid=500000435230 |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000009364502}}</ref>.
Some regions hold the event on different dates. Examples include: * '''[[:en:Warabi, Saitama|Warabi, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Toda, Saitama|Toda, Saitama]]''': February 8 and '''November 8'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Urawa-ku, Saitama|Urawa-ku, Saitama]]''' and '''[[:en:Ōshima, Tokyo|Oshima, Tokyo]] ([[:en:Izu Ōshima|Izu Ōshima]])''': February 8, '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Sōka|Sōka, Saitama]]''': January 8, February 8, '''March 8''', '''April 8''', and December 8<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Misato, Saitama (city)|Misato, Saitama]]''': February 8, April 8, '''July 8''', '''October 8''', and December 8 (not necessarily observed on all dates)<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Yamatsuri, Fukushima|Yamatsuri, Fukushima]]''': Held on the '''8th or 10th of each month'''<ref name=Cho/>. * '''[[:en:Hanawa, Fukushima|Hanawa, Fukushima]]''': '''Me-kago''' (eye baskets) are displayed on the 8th and taken down on the 10th<ref name=Cho/>.
== Customs == The event is associated with the visitation of '''yōkai''' (supernatural creatures) and evil spirits, which vary by region. are said to visit homes, and offerings such as '''red rice (sekihan)''', '''mochi''', and '''dango''' are made to welcome them.

Additionally, '''needle memorial services''' ([[:en:Hari-Kuyō|Needle Memorial Service]]) are conducted, particularly: * '''December 8''' in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}} * '''February 8''' in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]]<ref name="heibon">『{{ill|Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja|国民百科事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokumin Hyakka Jiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 Heibonsha, Vol. 6, pp.198–199</ref>.
Some regions observe '''taboos''', forbidding '''agricultural work or entering mountains''' on this day.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Hari-Kuyō|Needle Memorial Service]] * {{ill|Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|ja|正月事始め|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgatsu Koto Hajime|1=Shogatsu Jishime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545487}}
